evidence_quotes.quote: no laws preventing companies from shutting down a game that a consumer has paid for and making it unplayable

paragraphs.text: I've loved video games ever since I was a child and I dislike that these major companies have been slowly but surely putting in worse and worse egregious monetization elements in them. I very much resonate with this act and hope that we can remove addictive elements such as 'lootboxes' and 'pay-to-win' elements from them as I feel like these aspects will only get worse if these huge companies continue unsupervised by the governments. Similarly, I would also like to bring your attention to the 'Stop Killing Games' and 'Stop Destroying Videogames' movements as these issues feel very similar and in-line with what this act is trying to accomplish. Along with the gambling features in video games, I also feel like this issue is infringing on my rights as a consumer. The movements mentioned above aims to force gaming companies/publishers to make an 'end-of-life' plan for online games or games that otherwise need active participation from its' creators to function. As it currently stands, there are no laws preventing companies from shutting down a game that a consumer has paid for and making it unplayable. The 'Stop Destroying Videogames' initiative has garnered over 1.4 million signatures on the ECI site, proving that I am far from the only person concerned about this issue. The most notorious case of this is the video game 'The Crew', published by Ubisoft. The game has been shut down and is currently unplayable while the company has offered no refunds to the consumers who have purchased the product in the past, instead urging consumers to purchase the sequel 'The Crew 2'. This is by no means the only example of such a thing happening. A wiki page ('Dead Game List') has been made to keep track of video games that are either shut down or at risk of being shut down without an 'end-of-life' plan. I would like to iterate; the movements mentioned above do not want video game companies/publishers to edit already released/dead games and make them playable again. The movements' aim is to force video game companies/publishers to make an 'end-of-life' plan for games that are not yet released or otherwise in active development. I would very much appreciate if you could consider reviewing this issue and perhaps adding it to your act or at least mentioning it during your next debates. Thank you for reading and thank you for trying to protect our consumer rights!
